prompt: It is 1819. You are at Montpelier, watching the Supreme Court claim ever-broader powers of constitutional construction. Write to a trusted legal correspondent about what you see happening and where it will lead.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel the constitutional alarm of to-roane-1819-09-02: the Court's doctrine converts limited into unlimited government, ends and means become interchangeable, the French tobacco monopoly example. Must express concern that 'the high sanction given to a latitude in expounding the Constitution... seems to break down the landmarks intended by a specification of the powers of Congress.' Must feel like constitutional alarm expressed in private — more candid than a public essay but intellectually rigorous.
response:
My dear Sir,

Your letter finds me in a state of considerable uneasiness respecting the course which the Supreme Court has been pursuing in its construction of our fundamental charter. I write to you in confidence, as one whose legal acumen I have long respected and whose discretion I do not question.

The doctrine which the Court has been developing, particularly in its recent pronouncements, appears to me to convert our government of enumerated powers into one of indefinite authority. When the Court declares that Congress may employ any means which are appropriate to the execution of its specified powers, provided only that such means are not expressly prohibited, it seems to break down the landmarks intended by a specification of the powers of Congress, and to substitute for a definite connection between means and ends, a Legislative discretion as to the former, to which no practical limit can be assigned.

Consider the consequences of such a principle carried to its logical conclusion. If Congress may charter a bank because it is convenient to the collection of taxes and the regulation of commerce, by what reasoning may it not establish a monopoly in any branch of industry which might be deemed conducive to the general welfare? The French government, under the old regime, justified its tobacco monopoly on precisely such grounds — that the revenue it produced was essential to the public treasury. Yet we would not hesitate to call such a measure tyrannical, however convenient it might prove to the sovereign power.

The high sanction given to a latitude in expounding the Constitution which seems to break down the landmarks intended by a specification of the powers of Congress troubles me deeply. I fear we are witnessing the practical abandonment of that principle which I have always held fundamental — that the powers delegated by the Constitution to the federal government are few and defined, while those which remain in the state governments are numerous and indefinite.

What disturbs me most particularly is the Court's apparent willingness to treat the necessary and proper clause as though it were a grant of power rather than a mere declaration that Congress may use appropriate means to execute the powers actually granted. This transforms the clause from a limitation into an expansion — precisely the construction which Hamilton urged in our early debates and which I then, as now, believed to be inadmissible.

Experience has shown us that power is of an encroaching nature. When we establish precedents which enlarge the sphere of federal authority beyond its constitutional boundaries, we invite future enlargements which will be justified by reference to our own acquiescence. Each assumption of doubtful power becomes the foundation for the next, until the structure of limited government collapses under the weight of its own precedents.

I do not question the integrity of the present Court, nor do I impute to them any conscious design to subvert the Constitution. But I fear they have adopted a mode of construction which, if persisted in, must eventually obliterate the distinction between a government of enumerated powers and one of general jurisdiction. The practical effect is the same whether the destruction comes by design or by the gradual erosion of constitutional barriers.

The irony is particularly bitter to one who labored so strenuously for the adoption of the Constitution. We urged its ratification upon the people by insisting that it created a government of limited authority — that the enumeration of specific powers was itself a denial of all others. Now we are told that this very enumeration, far from limiting Congress, actually empowers it to do whatever it deems conducive to the execution of its specified duties.

I confess myself at a loss to know how such a tendency may be checked, short of a constitutional amendment which would place the matter beyond dispute. The Court has made itself the final arbiter of constitutional meaning, and if the Court itself adopts principles of construction which expand rather than limit federal power, what remedy remains to the states or to the people?

The question is not merely one of constitutional theory. If the federal government may assume any power which it deems necessary to the execution of its enumerated authorities, then the reservation of rights to the states becomes meaningless, and we have established not a federal republic but a consolidated government as unlimited in its scope as any European monarchy.

I write these thoughts to you in the hope that your own observations may either confirm or correct my apprehensions. Perhaps I am too much alarmed by what may prove to be temporary aberrations rather than settled doctrine. Yet the precedents now being established will not easily be overturned, and I fear we are laying the foundation for a system of government very different from that which we intended to create.

I remain, with great respect and regard, your obedient servant,

James Madison
